topic: Theme C: The Existence of God & Revelation
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Does accepting the Big Bang force someone to reject belief in God?
items.correct: No - many believers accept the Big Bang and argue God is the cause behind it, while atheists see no need for a cause beyond nature; the science itself does not settle the question.
items.misconception: The Big Bang theory disproves the existence of God.
items.random_wrong: The Big Bang is a religious doctrine found in Genesis.
items.why: Students treat science and belief as automatic rivals; Theme C requires showing how the same evidence is read both ways.

items.q: Did evolution end every form of the design argument?
items.correct: Not for everyone - evolution challenges Paley-style examples of designed organisms, but some believers relocate design to the laws and fine-tuning of the universe; the debate continues.
items.misconception: Darwin's theory made all design arguments impossible to hold.
items.random_wrong: Evolution was proposed as an argument for God's existence.
items.why: Evaluation marks come from showing the argument adapts and is contested, not from declaring one side's instant victory.

items.q: Do religious believers accept every claimed vision as genuine revelation?
items.correct: No - traditions test claims against scripture, teaching and their effects on the person's life; many claimed revelations are rejected as mistaken or fraudulent.
items.misconception: Religions automatically believe anyone who says they have seen God.
items.random_wrong: Visions are accepted only if they happen in a place of worship.
items.why: Students miss that religions themselves are often the strictest critics of revelation claims, which matters for evaluating special revelation.

items.q: What does the design argument conclude?
items.correct: Order and purpose in nature point to a designer - God
items.random_wrong: God cannot be known at all
items.why: It's Order and purpose in nature point to a designer - God. “The universe has no order” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In the First Cause argument, God is described as what?
items.correct: The uncaused cause that brought the universe into existence
items.misconception: The final event of the universe
items.random_wrong: A being caused by the Big Bang
items.why: It's The uncaused cause that brought the universe into existence. “The final event of the universe” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: God revealed indirectly to everyone, for example through nature and scripture
items.misconception: A vision granted to one person
items.random_wrong: A message given only to priests
items.why: The answer examiners want is: God revealed indirectly to everyone, for example through nature and scripture. “A vision granted to one person” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What did Hume argue about miracles?
items.correct: Testimony for a miracle is never strong enough to outweigh the evidence for the laws of nature
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Testimony for a miracle is never strong enough to outweigh the evidence for the laws of nature. “Miracles happen every day” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
